diagrams.title: Security Functional Requirements Taxonomy
diagrams.description: A hierarchical tree diagram showing the main categories of security functional requirements including Identification & Authentication, Access Control, Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Audit & Accountability, and Non-repudiation. Each category branches into specific requirements.
elements: Root node: Security Functional Requirements
Branch 1: Identification & Authentication (user identification, authentication factors, identity verification)
Branch 2: Access Control (authorization, permissions, role-based access, least privilege)
Branch 3: Confidentiality (data encryption, information classification, privacy measures)
Branch 4: Integrity (data validation, checksum verification, change management, tamper detection)
Branch 5: Availability (redundancy, fault tolerance, backup & recovery, DoS protection)
Branch 6: Audit & Accountability (logging, monitoring, event tracking, forensic capability)
Branch 7: Non-repudiation (digital signatures, transaction records, proof of origin/delivery)
diagrams.purpose: Explains the comprehensive categorization of security functional requirements and their interrelationships, helping stakeholders understand the scope and structure of security needs for information systems.

diagrams.title: Security Requirements Engineering Process
diagrams.description: A flow diagram illustrating the sequential process from context analysis through requirements elicitation, specification, verification, and validation to final security requirements documentation.
elements: Step 1: Context Analysis (system boundaries, asset identification, threat landscape)
Step 2: Requirements Elicitation (stakeholder interviews, threat modeling, compliance mapping)
Step 3: Requirements Specification (structured documentation, requirement categorization)
Step 4: Requirements Verification (consistency check, completeness analysis, priority assignment)
Step 5: Requirements Validation (acceptance testing, feasibility assessment)
Output: Security Requirements Specification Document
Feedback loops between stages for refinement
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the systematic approach to identifying, documenting, and validating security functional requirements, emphasizing the iterative nature of security requirements engineering.

diagrams.title: Security Control Mapping to Functional Requirements
diagrams.description: A matrix-style diagram showing how common security controls (technical, administrative, physical) map to specific functional security requirements, demonstrating the implementation pathway.
elements: Technical Controls column: MFA, encryption, firewalls, IDS/IPS, access control lists
Administrative Controls column: policies, procedures, training, background checks
Physical Controls column: locks, CCTV, badge access, secure facilities
Mapping lines to: Authentication, Access Control, Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability
Coverage indicators showing which controls satisfy which requirements
Legend showing control types and requirement categories
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates the relationship between security functional requirements and the controls implemented to satisfy them, helping security architects and auditors trace requirements to concrete safeguards.
